Escalation paths differ by product line; enterprise accounts are routed directly to a dedicated support engineer, while self-serve accounts are placed into a shared queue handled by the first available agent. New agents complete a two-week onboarding program that combines shadowing experienced staff with a series of scenario-based assessments before they are allowed to handle tickets independently. Support metrics, including average handle time and first-contact resolution rate, are reviewed in a weekly operations meeting attended by both support leadership and product management. Customers on annual contracts are assigned a named account manager who conducts quarterly check-ins to review open issues and gather feedback on product usage. When a customer submits a support ticket, it is first triaged by an automated classifier that assigns a priority level based on account tier, sentiment, and the presence of specific keywords associated with outages or billing disputes. After-hours coverage for enterprise customers is provided by a follow-the-sun rotation across three regional support hubs, ensuring continuous coverage without requiring any single team to staff overnight shifts. Refunds and credits above a certain threshold require a secondary approval from a team lead before they can be issued, in order to prevent unauthorized adjustments to customer accounts. Response time targets are tracked against a service level agreement that varies by plan, with enterprise customers guaranteed a first response within one hour during business hours. Agents have access to a shared knowledge base containing troubleshooting steps for common issues, which is kept up to date by a small team that reviews resolved tickets on a weekly basis. Customer satisfaction is measured through a post-resolution survey, and scores below a certain threshold automatically trigger a follow-up review by the support manager. Sentiment scores derived from ticket text are used not only for prioritization but also as an input to a churn-risk model reviewed by the customer success team. Bug reports originating from support tickets are deduplicated against existing engineering tickets using a fuzzy-matching process before a new issue is created. Recurring issues that affect multiple customers within a short window are flagged for the engineering team, who investigate whether the underlying cause is a bug, a configuration issue, or a documentation gap. Live chat sessions are staffed separately from the ticket queue, with a dynamic routing system that balances incoming chats across available agents based on current load. Support macros and canned responses are versioned centrally, so that updates to policy language are automatically reflected across every agent's workspace without manual redistribution. Tickets that remain unresolved past their SLA deadline are automatically escalated to a supervisor queue, where they receive priority handling until the issue is closed. Question: Summarize the document's description of how support quality is measured after a ticket is resolved.

Document:
Supplier performance is scored on a monthly basis using a composite of on-time delivery rate, order accuracy, and defect rate, and consistently underperforming suppliers are flagged for a formal review. Seasonal product lines are planned up to a year in advance, with initial purchase quantities based on prior-year sell-through rates adjusted for known trend shifts. Loss prevention teams review point-of-sale exception reports daily, looking for patterns such as excessive voids or discounts that may indicate internal shrink. Price changes are staged in a pricing engine before being pushed live, allowing merchandising teams to preview the impact on margin across different product categories before committing the change. Store layouts are periodically tested using controlled experiments across a subset of locations before being rolled out chain-wide, measuring the impact on both sales and average transaction size. Loyalty program points are accrued based on pre-tax purchase totals and are subject to an expiration policy that varies depending on the customer's membership tier. Markdowns are applied in stages according to a predefined clearance calendar, with the depth and timing of each markdown varying by category and current sell-through pace. Store staffing levels are planned using a forecasting model that incorporates historical foot traffic patterns, local events, and weather forecasts for the upcoming scheduling period. Omnichannel fulfillment allows online orders to be picked from the nearest store with available inventory, reducing shipping time and cost compared to fulfilling exclusively from a central warehouse. Category managers review a standard set of performance metrics each period, including sell-through rate, gross margin return on investment, and inventory turns, to inform future buying decisions. Returns processed in-store are cross-checked against the original transaction record to confirm eligibility, and items flagged as damaged are routed to a separate disposition queue rather than back into sellable inventory. New store openings follow a standardized playbook covering everything from initial inventory allocation to local marketing, with a dedicated launch team supporting the first eight weeks of operation. Warehouse pick paths are optimized on a recurring basis using historical order data, reducing the average time required to fulfill both store replenishment and direct-to-consumer orders. Vendor contracts include negotiated chargeback terms for late shipments, and these chargebacks are tracked automatically against each purchase order as it moves through receiving. Store replenishment orders are generated automatically based on a rolling average of recent sales velocity, seasonal adjustment factors, and any promotional events scheduled for the upcoming weeks. Inventory counts are reconciled nightly between the point-of-sale system and the central warehouse management system, with any discrepancies above a set tolerance flagged for manual review the following morning. Question: Using only the document, explain how supplier performance is evaluated and what happens to underperforming suppliers.

Document:
Escalation paths differ by product line; enterprise accounts are routed directly to a dedicated support engineer, while self-serve accounts are placed into a shared queue handled by the first available agent. New agents complete a two-week onboarding program that combines shadowing experienced staff with a series of scenario-based assessments before they are allowed to handle tickets independently. Support metrics, including average handle time and first-contact resolution rate, are reviewed in a weekly operations meeting attended by both support leadership and product management. Customers on annual contracts are assigned a named account manager who conducts quarterly check-ins to review open issues and gather feedback on product usage.
